name: B.S. in Science, Technology & Society and B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The information-systems spine is coherent and genuinely vertical (CS 100->113->114, IS 117->218->219->421, IS 331->IS 491 senior project) with a deepestPrereqChain of 7, and business courses (ACCT 115/117, ECON 201, FIN 218/315, HRM 301, ENTR 210, IE 492) interleave sensibly. However, only one plan-of-study term parsed (requirementGroups=1, the '2nd Semester' block), so term-over-term ordering cannot be verified, and for a dual degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society the STS/policy/social-studies-of-technology content is almost invisible in the course set, represented only by IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full eight-term plan-of-study grid and explicitly slot the STS half (history/philosophy of science, technology policy, science-and-society courses) so the dual degree reads as an integrated program rather than a BIS track with a missing companion.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and readable, with grade-conditioned prerequisites (e.g., 'CS 113 with a grade C or better', 'IS 219 and (IS 331 or CS 331)') and per-course depths from 0 to 7. missingCourseCount is only 3, and all three (YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are one-credit college seminars that dangle without breaking any core IS chain; the depth-7 path into IS 465/IS 448 is on the deep side but genuine.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the three YWCC 107/207/307 seminars so they resolve, and audit the depth-7 chain into IS 465 (statistics GUR -> ... ) to confirm it does not over-constrain scheduling in an already dual-major load.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or narrative overview of any kind; only the course list and a single parsed term are present. For a dual degree that fuses a humanities/social-science field (STS) with an applied IS field, the absence of outcomes is especially costly because the integrative value proposition is left entirely implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-7 program-level learning outcomes that explicitly bridge the two halves (e.g., analyze the societal and ethical impact of information systems, design and build business web/mobile applications, evaluate technology policy with data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: summedTermCredits is only 12.0 against a statedTotalCredits of 120.0, giving a creditReconcileDelta of -108.0 because just one 12-credit term parsed. Beyond the parsing gap, 120 credits is itself suspect for a genuine dual B.S./B.S. degree, which typically requires substantially more, so the reader cannot see how general-education, STS, business, and IS credits are apportioned.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the full credit grid and confirm the true total for the dual degree, then add a distribution summary (general education / STS core / business core / IS core / electives) so the 120 (or corrected) figure is transparent and reconciles term by term.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Only one term rendered (the '2nd Semester' group), several rows carry null course codes, and alternatives appear as bare text such as 'or IT 491 or IT Capstone Project'; there is no program description, no outcomes, and no STS-side structure. The single parsed block therefore reads as a fragment rather than a catalog-quality plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the plan-of-study parsing so all terms render with labeled headings and per-term credit totals, collapse 'or' alternatives into clear either/or pairs, and add a short program overview describing the STS+BIS pairing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The BIS half is current and employable (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IS 333 social network analysis, IS 390 requirements analysis, IS 455 IS management, IT 310 e-commerce, IS 247/375 UX), but for a program explicitly branded Science, Technology & Society the currency of the STS half is thin: only IS 350 touches ethics, and there is no data governance, technology policy, privacy law, or applied data-science/AI content despite this being exactly where STS+IS careers now concentrate.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or list electives in technology policy / data governance and applied data science or AI so the STS half carries modern currency (algorithmic accountability, privacy regulation) rather than resting on a single ethics course.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled courses carry real, specific descriptions rather than placeholders (e.g., ACCT 115 develops 'fundamentals of financial accounting'; IS 333 teaches programs that 'grab information from social networking systems ... and analyze this to reveal useful but hidden information'). Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters, and a few are genuinely thin (IS 489 INFO Undergrad Thesis Research at ~81 characters), but coverage is otherwise strong and informative.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in clear, human-readable form with explicit grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and the parser resolved a clean depth graph. The only blemish is the 3 dangling YWCC 107/207/307 seminar references, which are peripheral one-credit courses rather than gatekeepers.

missingTopics.topic: Technology and science policy / STS methods
missingTopics.rationale: For a degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society, the course set shows no policy analysis, history/philosophy of science, or qualitative social-science methods course; IS 350 alone cannot carry the STS half, so a dedicated STS core (e.g., a Technology, Policy and Society course) is needed.

missingTopics.topic: Applied data analytics and machine learning
missingTopics.rationale: The IS track stops at IS 333 social network analysis and IS 392 web mining; there is no applied ML, predictive modeling, or Python/R data-science course, which is now standard for information-systems graduates and directly relevant to an STS student studying data-driven society.

No cybersecurity, privacy, or data-governance course appears despite IS 218/322/331 building data-collecting web, mobile, and database systems; STS+BIS graduates working at the technology-society boundary need GDPR/CCPA-style privacy and security engineering literacy.

Only MATH 105 (elementary probability and statistics) is visible; an STS+IS program that analyzes technology in society should require inferential statistics and research design (e.g., MATH 333) so students can produce, not just consume, empirical claims.

The web sequence (IS 117/218/219/421) is traditional server-side web development with no cloud, DevOps, or API/microservice content, leaving a gap between the program's build courses and how business information systems are actually deployed today.

emergingTrends.rationale: This is the fastest-growing STS-meets-technology niche and the natural destination for STS+BIS graduates; the program should extend IS 350 into a dedicated responsible-AI / algorithmic-accountability course to own this space.

Privacy law (GDPR, CCPA, state AI acts) is reshaping how information systems are built and operated; a program that combines societal analysis with hands-on IS development is ideally positioned to teach privacy-by-design if it adds a privacy/compliance elective.

LLM-assisted development and AI-embedded business software are now baseline industry practice; IS 218/322/421 build courses should incorporate AI-assisted and AI-feature development so graduates ship current systems.

emergingTrends.trend: Platform and social-media analytics for policy
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 social network analysis is a strong foundation the program can grow into misinformation, platform governance, and computational social science, uniting the STS and IS halves around a marketable, high-demand skill set.

With IS 247/375/448 already present, the program can position for value-sensitive and ethical design, an area where STS framing plus UX practice is a distinctive combination employers increasingly seek.

topSuggestions: Rebuild the full plan-of-study grid; only one 12-credit term parsed (creditReconcileDelta -108 against a stated 120), so students currently cannot see the degree structure at all.
Make the Science, Technology & Society half explicit by slotting STS/policy/history-of-science courses, since the visible curriculum is almost entirely BIS with only IS 350 representing STS.
Publish program-level learning outcomes that bridge societal analysis and applied IS, none of which currently appear in the data.
Add modern data and policy currency: an applied data-science/ML course and a technology-policy or data-privacy course, closing the biggest STS-side and IS-side gaps at once.
Catalog the YWCC 107/207/307 seminars and verify the true dual-degree credit total so the page reconciles and dangling references disappear.
